content: We are stepping into the dangerous but powerful territory of function-like proc-macros, specifically those annotated with #[proc_macro], which act as true compile-time functions that return a TokenStream. The immediate mental block many developers hit is treating this like a regular Rust function returning a String or Vec; you cannot do string interpolation on tokens because the compiler has already parsed your input into an abstract syntax tree before your macro runs, meaning you never receive raw text to manipulate with standard string methods. Instead, your entry point must be a function returning TokenStream that accepts exactly one argument of type TokenStream representing the user's source code as a token stream. If you try to destructure this single TokenStream into named fields like sql::Query or html::Tag using a macro expansion rule without declaring #[proc_macro] on the function, the compiler will fail because it expects a single token stream input but finds multiple arguments in your function signature. The correct progression starts with a naive approach where you attempt to parse tokens manually using Tree-sitter or another parser library before passing them into your logic; this fails because you are duplicating work that the Rust compiler has already done, resulting in an inefficient and error-prone implementation that ignores the built-in token stream abstraction provided by the proc-macro crate. You must abandon the naive parsing strategy immediately and embrace the function-like model where your macro receives a single TokenStream parameter named tokens, then iterates through that stream using token iterators from the syn crate to reconstruct new tokens based on specific rules like SQL query construction or HTML tag generation. For example, when building a sql!{} macro, you cannot simply match against string literals because those are just placeholders for actual token sequences; instead, your function must iterate over the incoming TokenStream, identify keywords like SELECT, FROM, and WHERE as distinct tokens, and then emit new tokens representing the expanded query structure while preserving type information. The critical edge case that trips up experienced developers is the limitation of macro expansion rules: a proc-macro cannot inspect its own arguments recursively within the same expansion pass without risking infinite recursion or losing context; this means if your DSL requires conditional logic based on token types, you must implement stateful tracking using syn::Ident or syn::Symbol to maintain context across iterations rather than trying to evaluate complex expressions inside the macro body itself. Furthermore, you cannot rely on standard Rust control flow structures like if-else blocks for simple pattern matching when dealing with tokens; instead, you should use procedural macros designed specifically for token manipulation that leverage the TokenStream's internal structure through traits like syn::Token and syn::Parse to ensure your generated code remains valid Rust syntax. By following this path—from rejecting naive string-based approaches to adopting a strict function-like signature that leverages the single TokenStream argument—you build robust DSLs like sql!{} or html!{} that integrate seamlessly with the compiler's type system, ensuring that any errors are caught at compile time rather than runtime while maintaining the high-performance characteristics of zero-cost abstractions. This approach removes the mental block by enforcing a rigid contract: your function always returns TokenStream, accepts exactly one TokenStream argument, and never attempts to parse tokens twice, turning a confusing edge case into a predictable, authoritative pattern for advanced macro development.

When you first encounter the #[proc_macro] attribute in Rust, it feels like a black box because unlike C macros or Python decorators, it doesn't operate on text but rather on the compiler's internal representation of code known as a token stream. The idiomatic way to build these is by implementing a function that receives a Vec representing the macro arguments and returns another such vector, effectively acting as a DSL where you can parse user input into meaningful Rust types before emitting new tokens for compilation. This function-like proc macro operates within the context of the crate containing the attribute derivation or expansion, meaning if you define sql!{} in your app's crate, the implementation lives there alongside your application code rather than being distributed separately like a library macro in older systems. The input token stream is typically generated by the compiler from arguments passed to the macro invocation, so when you write html!{
...
} the macro receives tokens corresponding to those inner elements as a Vec, which it then parses into an AST using crates like syn or thiserror for error handling. Once parsed, your function can manipulate these tokens—perhaps validating them against schema rules or transforming them into intermediate representations—and finally return a new token stream that the compiler will insert at the macro site instead of the original call. This is fundamentally different from languages like C where macros are preprocessor directives operating on raw text before compilation begins; Rust's approach happens entirely within the compiler pipeline after parsing, allowing for type safety and semantic understanding that pure string replacement cannot achieve. For instance, if you want to implement a sql!{} macro that ensures all arguments are valid table names or column identifiers, you parse them into syn::Ident objects using TokenTree::into_token_stream, then perform logical validation before returning a Vec containing the generated query syntax tokens. The power of this pattern shines when building complex DSLs because you can leverage Rust's ownership model to manage temporary data structures during parsing without leaking or duplicating memory, something impossible in imperative languages where macros often rely on string concatenation and manual error propagation. You will notice that the return type must always be a token stream compatible with the macro context, meaning if your macro is used in an item position like #[derive], it returns tokens for the derived implementation, whereas if used inline, it returns tokens to replace the original expression. This strict typing ensures that every proc macro behaves predictably within the compilation flow, making Rust's function-like macros far more robust than their counterparts elsewhere. Finally, remember that while you can access the crate via syn::parse_macro_input or manual token iteration, the idiomatic approach favors explicit parsing with error recovery patterns using syn::Error and thiserror to provide context-aware diagnostics when users make mistakes in their DSL syntax.
